title: There Is No A.I.
excerpt: Jaron Lanier writes about how the term “artificial intelligence” is a misnomer, possibilities for productively using A.I., and the subject of data dignity.
content_html:
There are ways of controlling the new technology—but first we have to stop mythologizing it.

April 20, 2023

Illustration by Nicholas Konrad / The New Yorker

As a computer scientist, I don’t like the term “A.I.” In fact, I think it’s misleading—maybe even a little dangerous. Everybody’s already using the term, and it might seem a little late in the day to be arguing about it. But we’re at the beginning of a new technological era—and the easiest way to mismanage a technology is to misunderstand it.

The term “artificial intelligence” has a long history—it was coined in the nineteen-fifties, in the early days of computers. More recently, computer scientists have grown up on movies like “The Terminator” and “The Matrix,” and on characters like Commander Data, from “Star Trek: The Next Generation.” These cultural touchstones have become an almost religious mythology in tech culture. It’s only natural that computer scientists long to create A.I. and realize a long-held dream.

What’s striking, though, is that many of the people who are pursuing the A.I. dream also worry that it might mean doomsday for mankind. It is widely stated, even by scientists at the very center of today’s efforts, that what A.I. researchers are doing could result in the annihilation of our species, or at least in great harm to humanity, and soon. In a recent poll, half of A.I. scientists agreed that there was at least a ten-per-cent chance that the human race would be destroyed by A.I. Even my colleague and friend Sam Altman, who runs OpenAI, has made similar comments. Step into any Silicon Valley coffee shop and you can hear the same debate unfold: one person says that the new code is just code and that people are in charge, but another argues that anyone with this opinion just doesn’t get how profound the new tech is. The arguments aren’t entirely rational: when I ask my most fearful scientist friends to spell out how an A.I. apocalypse might happen, they often seize up from the paralysis that overtakes someone trying to conceive of infinity. They say things like “Accelerating progress will fly past us and we will not be able to conceive of what is happening.”

I don’t agree with this way of talking. Many of my friends and colleagues are deeply impressed by their experiences with the latest big models, like GPT-4, and are practically holding vigils to await the appearance of a deeper intelligence. My position is not that they are wrong but that we can’t be sure; we retain the option of classifying the software in different ways.

The most pragmatic position is to think of A.I. as a tool, not a creature. My attitude doesn’t eliminate the possibility of peril: however we think about it, we can still design and operate our new tech badly, in ways that can hurt us or even lead to our extinction. Mythologizing the technology only makes it more likely that we’ll fail to operate it well—and this kind of thinking limits our imaginations, tying them to yesterday’s dreams. We can work better under the assumption that there is no such thing as A.I. The sooner we understand this, the sooner we’ll start managing our new technology intelligently.

If the new tech isn’t true artificial intelligence, then what is it? In my view, the most accurate way to understand what we are building today is as an innovative form of social collaboration.

A program like OpenAI’s GPT-4, which can write sentences to order, is something like a version of Wikipedia that includes much more data, mashed together using statistics. Programs that create images to order are something like a version of online image search, but with a system for combining the pictures. In both cases, it’s people who have written the text and furnished the images. The new programs mash up work done by human minds. What’s innovative is that the mashup process has become guided and constrained, so that the results are usable and often striking. This is a significant achievement and worth celebrating—but it can be thought of as illuminating previously hidden concordances between human creations, rather than as the invention of a new mind.

As far as I can tell, my view flatters the technology. After all, what is civilization but social collaboration? Seeing A.I. as a way of working together, rather than as a technology for creating independent, intelligent beings, may make it less mysterious—less like HAL 9000 or Commander Data. But that’s good, because mystery only makes mismanagement more likely.

It’s easy to attribute intelligence to the new systems; they have a flexibility and unpredictability that we don’t usually associate with computer technology. But this flexibility arises from simple mathematics. A large language model like GPT-4 contains a cumulative record of how particular words coincide in the vast amounts of text that the program has processed. This gargantuan tabulation causes the system to intrinsically approximate many grammar patterns, along with aspects of what might be called authorial style. When you enter a query consisting of certain words in a certain order, your entry is correlated with what’s in the model; the results can come out a little differently each time, because of the complexity of correlating billions of entries.

The non-repeating nature of this process can make it feel lively. And there’s a sense in which it can make the new systems more human-centered. When you synthesize a new image with an A.I. tool, you may get a bunch of similar options and then have to choose from them; if you’re a student who uses an L.L.M. to cheat on an essay assignment, you might read options generated by the model and select one. A little human choice is demanded by a technology that is non-repeating.

Many of the uses of A.I. that I like rest on advantages we gain when computers get less rigid. Digital stuff as we have known it has a brittle quality that forces people to conform to it, rather than assess it. We’ve all endured the agony of watching some poor soul at a doctor’s office struggle to do the expected thing on a front-desk screen. The face contorts; humanity is undermined. The need to conform to digital designs has created an ambient expectation of human subservience. A positive spin on A.I. is that it might spell the end of this torture, if we use it well. We can now imagine a Web site that reformulates itself on the fly for someone who is color-blind, say, or a site that tailors itself to someone’s particular cognitive abilities and styles. A humanist like me wants people to have more control, rather than be overly influenced or guided by technology. Flexibility may give us back some agency.

Still, despite these possible upsides, it’s more than reasonable to worry that the new technology will push us around in ways we don’t like or understand. Recently, some friends of mine circulated a petition asking for a pause on the most ambitious A.I. development. The idea was that we’d work on policy during the pause. The petition was signed by some in our community but not others. I found the notion too hazy—what level of progress would mean that the pause could end? Every week, I receive new but always vague mission statements from organizations seeking to initiate processes to set A.I. policy.

These efforts are well intentioned, but they seem hopeless to me. For years, I worked on the E.U.’s privacy policies, and I came to realize that we don’t know what privacy is. It’s a term we use every day, and it can make sense in context, but we can’t nail it down well enough to generalize. The closest we have come to a definition of privacy is probably “the right to be left alone,” but that seems quaint in an age when we are constantly dependent on digital services. In the context of A.I., “the right to not be manipulated by computation” seems almost correct, but doesn’t quite say everything we’d like it to.

A.I.-policy conversations are dominated by terms like “alignment” (is what an A.I. “wants” aligned with what humans want?), “safety” (can we foresee guardrails that will foil a bad A.I.?), and “fairness” (can we forestall all the ways a program might treat certain people with disfavor?). The community has certainly accomplished much good by pursuing these ideas, but that hasn’t quelled our fears. We end up motivating people to try to circumvent the vague protections we set up. Even though the protections do help, the whole thing becomes a game—like trying to outwit a sneaky genie. The result is that the A.I.-research community communicates the warning that their creations might still kill all of humanity soon, while proposing ever more urgent, but turgid, deliberative processes.

Recently, I tried an informal experiment, calling colleagues and asking them if there’s anything specific on which we can all seem to agree. I’ve found that there is a foundation of agreement. We all seem to agree that deepfakes—false but real-seeming images, videos, and so on—should be labelled as such by the programs that create them. Communications coming from artificial people, and automated interactions that are designed to manipulate the thinking or actions of a human being, should be labelled as well. We also agree that these labels should come with actions that can be taken. People should be able to understand what they’re seeing, and should have reasonable choices in return.

How can all this be done? There is also near-unanimity, I find, that the black-box nature of our current A.I. tools must end. The systems must be made more transparent. We need to get better at saying what is going on inside them and why. This won’t be easy. The problem is that the large-model A.I. systems we are talking about aren’t made of explicit ideas. There is no definite representation of what the system “wants,” no label for when it is doing a particular thing, like manipulating a person. There is only a giant ocean of jello—a vast mathematical mixing. A writers’-rights group has proposed that real human authors be paid in full when tools like GPT are used in the scriptwriting process; after all, the system is drawing on scripts that real people have made. But when we use A.I. to produce film clips, and potentially whole movies, there won’t necessarily be a screenwriting phase. A movie might be produced that appears to have a script, soundtrack, and so on, but it will have been calculated into existence as a whole. Similarly, no sketch precedes the generation of a painting from an illustration A.I. Attempting to open the black box by making a system spit out otherwise unnecessary items like scripts, sketches, or intentions will involve building another black box to interpret the first—an infinite regress.

At the same time, it’s not true that the interior of a big model has to be a trackless wilderness. We may not know what an “idea” is from a formal, computational point of view, but there could be tracks made not of ideas but of people. At some point in the past, a real person created an illustration that was input as data into the model, and, in combination with contributions from other people, this was transformed into a fresh image. Big-model A.I. is made of people—and the way to open the black box is to reveal them.

This concept, which I’ve contributed to developing, is usually called “data dignity.” It appeared, long before the rise of big-model “A.I.,” as an alternative to the familiar arrangement in which people give their data for free in exchange for free services, such as internet searches or social networking. Data dignity is sometimes known as “data as labor” or “plurality research.” The familiar arrangement has turned out to have a dark side: because of “network effects,” a few platforms take over, eliminating smaller players, like local newspapers. Worse, since the immediate online experience is supposed to be free, the only remaining business is the hawking of influence. Users experience what seems to be a communitarian paradise, but they are targeted by stealthy and addictive algorithms that make people vain, irritable, and paranoid.

In a world with data dignity, digital stuff would typically be connected with the humans who want to be known for having made it. In some versions of the idea, people could get paid for what they create, even when it is filtered and recombined through big models, and tech hubs would earn fees for facilitating things that people want to do. Some people are horrified by the idea of capitalism online, but this would be a more honest capitalism. The familiar “free” arrangement has been a disaster.

One of the reasons the tech community worries that A.I. could be an existential threat is that it could be used to toy with people, just as the previous wave of digital technologies have been. Given the power and potential reach of these new systems, it’s not unreasonable to fear extinction as a possible result. Since that danger is widely recognized, the arrival of big-model A.I. could be an occasion to reformat the tech industry for the better.

Implementing data dignity will require technical research and policy innovation. In that sense, the subject excites me as a scientist. Opening the black box will only make the models more interesting. And it might help us understand more about language, which is the human invention that truly impresses, and the one that we are still exploring after all these hundreds of thousands of years.

Could data dignity address the economic worries that are often expressed about A.I.? The main concern is that workers will be devalued or displaced. Publicly, techies will sometimes say that, in the coming years, people who work with A.I. will be more productive and will find new types of jobs in a more productive economy. (A worker might become a prompt engineer for A.I. programs, for instance—someone who collaborates with or controls an A.I.) And yet, in private, the same people will quite often say, “No, A.I. will overtake this idea of collaboration.” No more remuneration for today’s accountants, radiologists, truck drivers, writers, film directors, or musicians.

A data-dignity approach would trace the most unique and influential contributors when a big model provides a valuable output. For instance, if you ask a model for “an animated movie of my kids in an oil-painting world of talking cats on an adventure,” then certain key oil painters, cat portraitists, voice actors, and writers—or their estates—might be calculated to have been uniquely essential to the creation of the new masterpiece. They would be acknowledged and motivated. They might even get paid.

There is a fledgling data-dignity research community, and here is an example of a debate within it: How detailed an accounting should data dignity attempt? Not everyone agrees. The system wouldn’t necessarily account for the billions of people who have made ambient contributions to big models—those who have added to a model’s simulated competence with grammar, for example. At first, data dignity might attend only to the small number of special contributors who emerge in a given situation. Over time, though, more people might be included, as intermediate rights organizations—unions, guilds, professional groups, and so on—start to play a role. People in the data-dignity community sometimes call these anticipated groups mediators of individual data (MIDs) or data trusts. People need collective-bargaining power to have value in an online world—especially when they might get lost in a giant A.I. model. And when people share responsibility in a group, they self-police, reducing the need, or temptation, for governments and companies to censor or control from above. Acknowledging the human essence of big models might lead to a blossoming of new positive social institutions.

Data dignity is not just for white-collar roles. Consider what might happen if A.I.-driven tree-trimming robots are introduced. Human tree trimmers might find themselves devalued or even out of work. But the robots could eventually allow for a new type of indirect landscaping artistry. Some former workers, or others, might create inventive approaches—holographic topiary, say, that looks different from different angles—that find their way into the tree-trimming models. With data dignity, the models might create new sources of income, distributed through collective organizations. Tree trimming would become more multifunctional and interesting over time; there would be a community motivated to remain valuable. Each new successful introduction of an A.I. or robotic application could involve the inauguration of a new kind of creative work. In ways large and small, this could help ease the transition to an economy into which models are integrated.

Many people in Silicon Valley see universal basic income as a solution to potential economic problems created by A.I. But U.B.I. amounts to putting everyone on the dole in order to preserve the idea of black-box artificial intelligence. This is a scary idea, I think, in part because bad actors will want to seize the centers of power in a universal welfare system, as in every communist experiment. I doubt that data dignity could ever grow enough to sustain all of society, but I doubt that any social or economic principle will ever be complete. Whenever possible, the goal should be to at least establish a new creative class instead of a new dependent class.

There are also non-altruistic reasons for A.I. companies to embrace data dignity. The models are only as good as their inputs. It’s only through a system like data dignity that we can expand the models into new frontiers. Right now, it’s much easier to get an L.L.M. to write an essay than it is to ask the program to generate an interactive virtual-reality world, because there are very few virtual worlds in existence. Why not solve that problem by giving people who add more virtual worlds a chance for prestige and income?

Could data dignity help with any of the human-annihilation scenarios? A big model could make us incompetent, or confuse us so much that our society goes collectively off the rails; a powerful, malevolent person could use A.I. to do us all great harm; and some people also think that the model itself could “jailbreak,” taking control of our machines or weapons and using them against us.

We can find precedents for some of these scenarios not just in science fiction but in more ordinary market and technology failures. An example is the 2019 catastrophe related to Boeing’s 737 MAX jets. The planes included a flight-path-correction feature that in some cases fought the pilots, causing two mass-casualty crashes. The problem was not the technology in isolation but the way that it was integrated into the sales cycle, training sessions, user interface, and documentation. Pilots thought that they were doing the right thing by trying to counteract the system in certain circumstances, but they were doing exactly the wrong thing, and they had no way of knowing. Boeing failed to communicate clearly about how the technology worked, and the resulting confusion led to disaster.

Anything engineered—cars, bridges, buildings—can cause harm to people, and yet we have built a civilization on engineering. It’s by increasing and broadening human awareness, responsibility, and participation that we can make automation safe; conversely, if we treat our inventions as occult objects, we can hardly be good engineers. Seeing A.I. as a form of social collaboration is more actionable: it gives us access to the engine room, which is made of people.

Let’s consider the apocalyptic scenario in which A.I. drives our society off the rails. One way this could happen is through deepfakes. Suppose that an evil person, perhaps working in an opposing government on a war footing, decides to stoke mass panic by sending all of us convincing videos of our loved ones being tortured or abducted from our homes. (The data necessary to create such videos are, in many cases, easy to obtain through social media or other channels.) Chaos would ensue, even if it soon became clear that the videos were faked. How could we prevent such a scenario? The answer is obvious: digital information must have context. Any collection of bits needs a history. When you lose context, you lose control.

Why don’t bits come attached to the stories of their origins? There are many reasons. The original design of the Web didn’t keep track of where bits came from, likely to make it easier for the network to grow quickly. (Computers and bandwidth were poor in the beginning.) Why didn’t we start remembering where bits came from when it became more feasible to at least approximate digital provenance? It always felt to me that we wanted the Web to be more mysterious than it needed to be. Whatever the reason, the Web was made to remember everything while forgetting its context.

Today, most people take it for granted that the Web, and indeed the Internet it is built on, is, by its nature, anti-contextual and devoid of provenance. We assume that decontextualization is intrinsic to the very idea of a digital network. That was never so, however; the initial proposals for digital-network architecture, put forward by the monumental scientist Vannevar Bush in 1945 and the computer scientist Ted Nelson in 1960, preserved provenance. Now A.I. is revealing the true costs of ignoring this approach. Without provenance, we have no way of controlling our A.I.s, or of making them economically fair. And this risks pushing our society to the brink.

If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why? Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera. We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it. Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available? There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.

The technical challenges of data dignity are real and must inspire serious scientific ambition. The policy challenges would also be substantial—a sign, perhaps, that they are meaningful and concrete. But we need to change the way we think, and to embrace the hard work of renovation. By persisting with the ideas of the past—among them, a fascination with the possibility of an A.I. that lives independently of the people who contribute to it—we risk using our new technologies in ways that make the world worse. If society, economics, culture, technology, or any other spheres of activity are to serve people, that can only be because we decide that people enjoy a special status to be served.

This is my plea to all my colleagues. Think of people. People are the answer to the problems of bits. ♦

  • A scientist with a Ph.D. from Harvard fatally shot three of her colleagues. Then revelations about her family history came to light.

Jaron Lanier is a computer scientist, author, and musician. He is presently “Prime Unifying Scientist” at Microsoft, but does not speak for the company.

In some versions of the idea, people could get paid for what they create, even when it is filtered and recombined through big models, and tech hubs would earn fees for facilitating things that people want to do. Some people are horrified by the idea of capitalism online, but this would be a more honest capitalism. The familiar “free” arrangement has been a disaster.One of the reasons the tech community worries that A.I. could be an existential threat is that it could be used to toy with people, just as the previous wave of digital technologies have been. Given the power and potential reach of these new systems, it’s not unreasonable to fear extinction as a possible result. Since that danger is widely recognized, the arrival of big-model A.I. could be an occasion to reformat the tech industry for the better.Implementing data dignity will require technical research and policy innovation. In that sense, the subject excites me as a scientist. Opening the black box will only make the models more interesting. And it might help us understand more about language, which is the human invention that truly impresses, and the one that we are still exploring after all these hundreds of thousands of years.Could data dignity address the economic worries that are often expressed about A.I.? The main concern is that workers will be devalued or displaced. Publicly, techies will sometimes say that, in the coming years, people who work with A.I. will be more productive and will find new types of jobs in a more productive economy. (A worker might become a prompt engineer for A.I. programs, for instance—someone who collaborates with or controls an A.I.) And yet, in private, the same people will quite often say, “No, A.I. will overtake this idea of collaboration.” No more remuneration for today’s accountants, radiologists, truck drivers, writers, film directors, or musicians.A data-dignity approach would trace the most unique and influential contributors when a big model provides a valuable output. For instance, if you ask a model for “an animated movie of my kids in an oil-painting world of talking cats on an adventure,” then certain key oil painters, cat portraitists, voice actors, and writers—or their estates—might be calculated to have been uniquely essential to the creation of the new masterpiece. They would be acknowledged and motivated. They might even get paid.There is a fledgling data-dignity research community, and here is an example of a debate within it: How detailed an accounting should data dignity attempt? Not everyone agrees. The system wouldn’t necessarily account for the billions of people who have made ambient contributions to big models—those who have added to a model’s simulated competence with grammar, for example. At first, data dignity might attend only to the small number of special contributors who emerge in a given situation. Over time, though, more people might be included, as intermediate rights organizations—unions, guilds, professional groups, and so on—start to play a role. People in the data-dignity community sometimes call these anticipated groups mediators of individual data (MIDs) or data trusts. People need collective-bargaining power to have value in an online world—especially when they might get lost in a giant A.I. model. And when people share responsibility in a group, they self-police, reducing the need, or temptation, for governments and companies to censor or control from above. Acknowledging the human essence of big models might lead to a blossoming of new positive social institutions.Data dignity is not just for white-collar roles. Consider what might happen if A.I.-driven tree-trimming robots are introduced. Human tree trimmers might find themselves devalued or even out of work. But the robots could eventually allow for a new type of indirect landscaping artistry. Some former workers, or others, might create inventive approaches—holographic topiary, say, that looks different from different angles—that find their way into the tree-trimming models. With data dignity, the models might create new sources of income, distributed through collective organizations. Tree trimming would become more multifunctional and interesting over time; there would be a community motivated to remain valuable. Each new successful introduction of an A.I. or robotic application could involve the inauguration of a new kind of creative work. In ways large and small, this could help ease the transition to an economy into which models are integrated.Many people in Silicon Valley see universal basic income as a solution to potential economic problems created by A.I. But U.B.I. amounts to putting everyone on the dole in order to preserve the idea of black-box artificial intelligence. This is a scary idea, I think, in part because bad actors will want to seize the centers of power in a universal welfare system, as in every communist experiment. I doubt that data dignity could ever grow enough to sustain all of society, but I doubt that any social or economic principle will ever be complete. Whenever possible, the goal should be to at least establish a new creative class instead of a new dependent class.There are also non-altruistic reasons for A.I. companies to embrace data dignity. The models are only as good as their inputs. It’s only through a system like data dignity that we can expand the models into new frontiers. Right now, it’s much easier to get an L.L.M. to write an essay than it is to ask the program to generate an interactive virtual-reality world, because there are very few virtual worlds in existence. Why not solve that problem by giving people who add more virtual worlds a chance for prestige and income?Could data dignity help with any of the human-annihilation scenarios? A big model could make us incompetent, or confuse us so much that our society goes collectively off the rails; a powerful, malevolent person could use A.I. to do us all great harm; and some people also think that the model itself could “jailbreak,” taking control of our machines or weapons and using them against us.We can find precedents for some of these scenarios not just in science fiction but in more ordinary market and technology failures. An example is the 2019 catastrophe related to Boeing’s 737 MAX jets. The planes included a flight-path-correction feature that in some cases fought the pilots, causing two mass-casualty crashes. The problem was not the technology in isolation but the way that it was integrated into the sales cycle, training sessions, user interface, and documentation. Pilots thought that they were doing the right thing by trying to counteract the system in certain circumstances, but they were doing exactly the wrong thing, and they had no way of knowing. Boeing failed to communicate clearly about how the technology worked, and the resulting confusion led to disaster.Anything engineered—cars, bridges, buildings—can cause harm to people, and yet we have built a civilization on engineering. It’s by increasing and broadening human awareness, responsibility, and participation that we can make automation safe; conversely, if we treat our inventions as occult objects, we can hardly be good engineers. Seeing A.I. as a form of social collaboration is more actionable: it gives us access to the engine room, which is made of people.Let’s consider the apocalyptic scenario in which A.I. drives our society off the rails. One way this could happen is through deepfakes. Suppose that an evil person, perhaps working in an opposing government on a war footing, decides to stoke mass panic by sending all of us convincing videos of our loved ones being tortured or abducted from our homes. (The data necessary to create such videos are, in many cases, easy to obtain through social media or other channels.) Chaos would ensue, even if it soon became clear that the videos were faked. How could we prevent such a scenario? The answer is obvious: digital information must have context. Any collection of bits needs a history. When you lose context, you lose control.Why don’t bits come attached to the stories of their origins? There are many reasons. The original design of the Web didn’t keep track of where bits came from, likely to make it easier for the network to grow quickly. (Computers and bandwidth were poor in the beginning.) Why didn’t we start remembering where bits came from when it became more feasible to at least approximate digital provenance? It always felt to me that we wanted the Web to be more mysterious than it needed to be. Whatever the reason, the Web was made to remember everything while forgetting its context.Today, most people take it for granted that the Web, and indeed the Internet it is built on, is, by its nature, anti-contextual and devoid of provenance. We assume that decontextualization is intrinsic to the very idea of a digital network. That was never so, however; the initial proposals for digital-network architecture, put forward by the monumental scientist Vannevar Bush in 1945 and the computer scientist Ted Nelson in 1960, preserved provenance. Now A.I. is revealing the true costs of ignoring this approach. Without provenance, we have no way of controlling our A.I.s, or of making them economically fair. And this risks pushing our society to the brink.If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why? Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera. We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it. Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available? There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.The technical challenges of data dignity are real and must inspire serious scientific ambition. The policy challenges would also be substantial—a sign, perhaps, that they are meaningful and concrete. But we need to change the way we think, and to embrace the hard work of renovation. By persisting with the ideas of the past—among them, a fascination with the possibility of an A.I. that lives independently of the people who contribute to it—we risk using our new technologies in ways that make the world worse. If society, economics, culture, technology, or any other spheres of activity are to serve people, that can only be because we decide that people enjoy a special status to be served.This is my plea to all my colleagues. Think of people. People are the answer to the problems of bits. ♦A scientist with a Ph.D. from Harvard fatally shot three of her colleagues. Then revelations about her family history came to light.Jaron Lanier is a computer scientist, author, and musician. He is presently “Prime Unifying Scientist” at Microsoft, but does not speak for the company.

blocks.html_en: As a computer scientist, I don’t like the term “A.I.” In fact, I think it’s misleading—maybe even a little dangerous. Everybody’s already using the term, and it might seem a little late in the day to be arguing about it. But we’re at the beginning of a new technological era—and the easiest way to mismanage a technology is to misunderstand it.

blocks.html_en: The term “artificial intelligence” has a long history—it was coined in the nineteen-fifties, in the early days of computers. More recently, computer scientists have grown up on movies like “The Terminator” and “The Matrix,” and on characters like Commander Data, from “Star Trek: The Next Generation.” These cultural touchstones have become an almost religious mythology in tech culture. It’s only natural that computer scientists long to create A.I. and realize a long-held dream.

blocks.html_en: What’s striking, though, is that many of the people who are pursuing the A.I. dream also worry that it might mean doomsday for mankind. It is widely stated, even by scientists at the very center of today’s efforts, that what A.I. researchers are doing could result in the annihilation of our species, or at least in great harm to humanity, and soon. In a recent poll, half of A.I. scientists agreed that there was at least a ten-per-cent chance that the human race would be destroyed by A.I. Even my colleague and friend Sam Altman, who runs OpenAI, has made similar comments. Step into any Silicon Valley coffee shop and you can hear the same debate unfold: one person says that the new code is just code and that people are in charge, but another argues that anyone with this opinion just doesn’t get how profound the new tech is. The arguments aren’t entirely rational: when I ask my most fearful scientist friends to spell out how an A.I. apocalypse might happen, they often seize up from the paralysis that overtakes someone trying to conceive of infinity. They say things like “Accelerating progress will fly past us and we will not be able to conceive of what is happening.”

blocks.html_en: I don’t agree with this way of talking. Many of my friends and colleagues are deeply impressed by their experiences with the latest big models, like GPT-4, and are practically holding vigils to await the appearance of a deeper intelligence. My position is not that they are wrong but that we can’t be sure; we retain the option of classifying the software in different ways.

sentences.zh: 我不同意这种说话方式。

sentences.zh: 我的立场不是他们错了，而是我们不能确定；我们保留以不同方式对软件进行分类的选项。

blocks.html_en: The most pragmatic position is to think of A.I. as a tool, not a creature. My attitude doesn’t eliminate the possibility of peril: however we think about it, we can still design and operate our new tech badly, in ways that can hurt us or even lead to our extinction. Mythologizing the technology only makes it more likely that we’ll fail to operate it well—and this kind of thinking limits our imaginations, tying them to yesterday’s dreams. We can work better under the assumption that there is no such thing as A.I. The sooner we understand this, the sooner we’ll start managing our new technology intelligently.

blocks.html_en: If the new tech isn’t true artificial intelligence, then what is it? In my view, the most accurate way to understand what we are building today is as an innovative form of social collaboration.

sentences.en: In my view, the most accurate way to understand what we are building today is as an innovative form of social collaboration.
sentences.zh: 在我看来，理解我们今天正在建设的东西的最准确方法是将其视为一种创新的社会协作形式。

blocks.html_en: A program like OpenAI’s GPT-4, which can write sentences to order, is something like a version of Wikipedia that includes much more data, mashed together using statistics. Programs that create images to order are something like a version of online image search, but with a system for combining the pictures. In both cases, it’s people who have written the text and furnished the images. The new programs mash up work done by human minds. What’s innovative is that the mashup process has become guided and constrained, so that the results are usable and often striking. This is a significant achievement and worth celebrating—but it can be thought of as illuminating previously hidden concordances between human creations, rather than as the invention of a new mind.

blocks.html_en: As far as I can tell, my view flatters the technology. After all, what is civilization but social collaboration? Seeing A.I. as a way of working together, rather than as a technology for creating independent, intelligent beings, may make it less mysterious—less like HAL 9000 or Commander Data. But that’s good, because mystery only makes mismanagement more likely.

blocks.html_en: It’s easy to attribute intelligence to the new systems; they have a flexibility and unpredictability that we don’t usually associate with computer technology. But this flexibility arises from simple mathematics. A large language model like GPT-4 contains a cumulative record of how particular words coincide in the vast amounts of text that the program has processed. This gargantuan tabulation causes the system to intrinsically approximate many grammar patterns, along with aspects of what might be called authorial style. When you enter a query consisting of certain words in a certain order, your entry is correlated with what’s in the model; the results can come out a little differently each time, because of the complexity of correlating billions of entries.

A little human choice is demanded by a technology that is non-repeating.

blocks.html_en: Many of the uses of A.I. that I like rest on advantages we gain when computers get less rigid. Digital stuff as we have known it has a brittle quality that forces people to conform to it, rather than assess it. We’ve all endured the agony of watching some poor soul at a doctor’s office struggle to do the expected thing on a front-desk screen. The face contorts; humanity is undermined. The need to conform to digital designs has created an ambient expectation of human subservience. A positive spin on A.I. is that it might spell the end of this torture, if we use it well. We can now imagine a Web site that reformulates itself on the fly for someone who is color-blind, say, or a site that tailors itself to someone’s particular cognitive abilities and styles. A humanist like me wants people to have more control, rather than be overly influenced or guided by technology. Flexibility may give us back some agency.

blocks.html_en: Still, despite these possible upsides, it’s more than reasonable to worry that the new technology will push us around in ways we don’t like or understand. Recently, some friends of mine circulated a petition asking for a pause on the most ambitious A.I. development. The idea was that we’d work on policy during the pause. The petition was signed by some in our community but not others. I found the notion too hazy—what level of progress would mean that the pause could end? Every week, I receive new but always vague mission statements from organizations seeking to initiate processes to set A.I. policy.

blocks.html_en: These efforts are well intentioned, but they seem hopeless to me. For years, I worked on the E.U.’s privacy policies, and I came to realize that we don’t know what privacy is. It’s a term we use every day, and it can make sense in context, but we can’t nail it down well enough to generalize. The closest we have come to a definition of privacy is probably “the right to be left alone,” but that seems quaint in an age when we are constantly dependent on digital services. In the context of A.I., “the right to not be manipulated by computation” seems almost correct, but doesn’t quite say everything we’d like it to.

blocks.html_en: A.I.-policy conversations are dominated by terms like “alignment” (is what an A.I. “wants” aligned with what humans want?), “safety” (can we foresee guardrails that will foil a bad A.I.?), and “fairness” (can we forestall all the ways a program might treat certain people with disfavor?). The community has certainly accomplished much good by pursuing these ideas, but that hasn’t quelled our fears. We end up motivating people to try to circumvent the vague protections we set up. Even though the protections do help, the whole thing becomes a game—like trying to outwit a sneaky genie. The result is that the A.I.-research community communicates the warning that their creations might still kill all of humanity soon, while proposing ever more urgent, but turgid, deliberative processes.

blocks.html_en: Recently, I tried an informal experiment, calling colleagues and asking them if there’s anything specific on which we can all seem to agree. I’ve found that there is a foundation of agreement. We all seem to agree that deepfakes—false but real-seeming images, videos, and so on—should be labelled as such by the programs that create them. Communications coming from artificial people, and automated interactions that are designed to manipulate the thinking or actions of a human being, should be labelled as well. We also agree that these labels should come with actions that can be taken. People should be able to understand what they’re seeing, and should have reasonable choices in return.

blocks.html_en: How can all this be done? There is also near-unanimity, I find, that the black-box nature of our current A.I. tools must end. The systems must be made more transparent. We need to get better at saying what is going on inside them and why. This won’t be easy. The problem is that the large-model A.I. systems we are talking about aren’t made of explicit ideas. There is no definite representation of what the system “wants,” no label for when it is doing a particular thing, like manipulating a person. There is only a giant ocean of jello—a vast mathematical mixing. A writers’-rights group has proposed that real human authors be paid in full when tools like GPT are used in the scriptwriting process; after all, the system is drawing on scripts that real people have made. But when we use A.I. to produce film clips, and potentially whole movies, there won’t necessarily be a screenwriting phase. A movie might be produced that appears to have a script, soundtrack, and so on, but it will have been calculated into existence as a whole. Similarly, no sketch precedes the generation of a painting from an illustration A.I. Attempting to open the black box by making a system spit out otherwise unnecessary items like scripts, sketches, or intentions will involve building another black box to interpret the first—an infinite regress.

blocks.html_en: At the same time, it’s not true that the interior of a big model has to be a trackless wilderness. Big-model A.I. is made of people—and the way to open the black box is to reveal them.

blocks.html_en: This concept, which I’ve contributed to developing, is usually called “data dignity.” It appeared, long before the rise of big-model “A.I.,” as an alternative to the familiar arrangement in which people give their data for free in exchange for free services, such as internet searches or social networking. Data dignity is sometimes known as “data as labor” or “plurality research.” The familiar arrangement has turned out to have a dark side: because of “network effects,” a few platforms take over, eliminating smaller players, like local newspapers. Worse, since the immediate online experience is supposed to be free, the only remaining business is the hawking of influence. Users experience what seems to be a communitarian paradise, but they are targeted by stealthy and addictive algorithms that make people vain, irritable, and paranoid.

blocks.html_en: In a world with data dignity, digital stuff would typically be connected with the humans who want to be known for having made it. In some versions of the idea, people could get paid for what they create, even when it is filtered and recombined through big models, and tech hubs would earn fees for facilitating things that people want to do. Some people are horrified by the idea of capitalism online, but this would be a more honest capitalism. The familiar “free” arrangement has been a disaster.

blocks.html_en: One of the reasons the tech community worries that A.I. could be an existential threat is that it could be used to toy with people, just as the previous wave of digital technologies have been. Since that danger is widely recognized, the arrival of big-model A.I. could be an occasion to reformat the tech industry for the better.

sentences.en: No more remuneration for today’s accountants, radiologists, truck drivers, writers, film directors, or musicians.
sentences.zh: 今天的会计师、放射科医生、卡车司机、作家、电影导演或音乐家不再有报酬。

blocks.html_en: A data-dignity approach would trace the most unique and influential contributors when a big model provides a valuable output. For instance, if you ask a model for “an animated movie of my kids in an oil-painting world of talking cats on an adventure,” then certain key oil painters, cat portraitists, voice actors, and writers—or their estates—might be calculated to have been uniquely essential to the creation of the new masterpiece. They would be acknowledged and motivated. They might even get paid.

sentences.en: A data-dignity approach would trace the most unique and influential contributors when a big model provides a valuable output.
sentences.zh: 当大模型提供有价值的输出时，数据尊严方法将追踪最独特和最有影响力的贡献者。

sentences.en: For instance, if you ask a model for “an animated movie of my kids in an oil-painting world of talking cats on an adventure,” then certain key oil painters, cat portraitists, voice actors, and writers—or their estates—might be calculated to have been uniquely essential to the creation of the new masterpiece.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果你要求一个模型“我的孩子们在油画世界中冒险的会说话的猫的动画电影”，那么某些重要的油画家、猫肖像画家、配音演员和作家——或者他们的财产——可能会被认为对新杰作的创作至关重要。

sentences.en: They would be acknowledged and motivated.
sentences.zh: 他们会得到认可和激励。

sentences.en: They might even get paid.
sentences.zh: 他们甚至可能会得到报酬。

blocks.html_en: There is a fledgling data-dignity research community, and here is an example of a debate within it: How detailed an accounting should data dignity attempt? Not everyone agrees. The system wouldn’t necessarily account for the billions of people who have made ambient contributions to big models—those who have added to a model’s simulated competence with grammar, for example. At first, data dignity might attend only to the small number of special contributors who emerge in a given situation. Over time, though, more people might be included, as intermediate rights organizations—unions, guilds, professional groups, and so on—start to play a role. People in the data-dignity community sometimes call these anticipated groups mediators of individual data (MIDs) or data trusts. People need collective-bargaining power to have value in an online world—especially when they might get lost in a giant A.I. model. And when people share responsibility in a group, they self-police, reducing the need, or temptation, for governments and companies to censor or control from above. Acknowledging the human essence of big models might lead to a blossoming of new positive social institutions.

sentences.en: There is a fledgling data-dignity research community, and here is an example of a debate within it: How detailed an accounting should data dignity attempt?
sentences.zh: 有一个刚刚起步的数据尊严研究社区，这里有一个内部辩论的例子：数据尊严应该尝试多详细的核算？

sentences.zh: 并非所有人都同意。

sentences.zh: 不过，随着时间的推移，随着中间权利组织（工会、行会、专业团体等）开始发挥作用，更多的人可能会被纳入其中。

blocks.html_en: Data dignity is not just for white-collar roles. Consider what might happen if A.I.-driven tree-trimming robots are introduced. Human tree trimmers might find themselves devalued or even out of work. But the robots could eventually allow for a new type of indirect landscaping artistry. Some former workers, or others, might create inventive approaches—holographic topiary, say, that looks different from different angles—that find their way into the tree-trimming models. With data dignity, the models might create new sources of income, distributed through collective organizations. Tree trimming would become more multifunctional and interesting over time; there would be a community motivated to remain valuable. Each new successful introduction of an A.I. or robotic application could involve the inauguration of a new kind of creative work. In ways large and small, this could help ease the transition to an economy into which models are integrated.

sentences.zh: 数据尊严不仅仅属于白领角色。

sentences.en: Consider what might happen if A.I.-driven tree-trimming robots are introduced.
sentences.zh: 考虑一下如果引入人工智能驱动的树木修剪机器人会发生什么。

blocks.html_en: Many people in Silicon Valley see universal basic income as a solution to potential economic problems created by A.I. But U.B.I. amounts to putting everyone on the dole in order to preserve the idea of black-box artificial intelligence. This is a scary idea, I think, in part because bad actors will want to seize the centers of power in a universal welfare system, as in every communist experiment. I doubt that data dignity could ever grow enough to sustain all of society, but I doubt that any social or economic principle will ever be complete. Whenever possible, the goal should be to at least establish a new creative class instead of a new dependent class.

sentences.zh: 硅谷的许多人将全民基本收入视为人工智能造成的潜在经济问题的解决方案。

sentences.en: But U.B.I.

sentences.zh: 我认为，这是一个可怕的想法，部分原因是坏人会想要夺取普遍福利体系中的权力中心，就像每一次共产主义实验一样。

sentences.zh: 只要有可能，目标至少应该是建立一个新的创意类，而不是一个新的依赖类。

blocks.html_en: There are also non-altruistic reasons for A.I. companies to embrace data dignity. The models are only as good as their inputs. It’s only through a system like data dignity that we can expand the models into new frontiers. Right now, it’s much easier to get an L.L.M. to write an essay than it is to ask the program to generate an interactive virtual-reality world, because there are very few virtual worlds in existence. Why not solve that problem by giving people who add more virtual worlds a chance for prestige and income?

sentences.en: There are also non-altruistic reasons for A.I.
sentences.zh: 人工智能也有非利他的原因。

sentences.en: companies to embrace data dignity.
sentences.zh: 企业拥抱数据尊严。

sentences.en: The models are only as good as their inputs.
sentences.zh: 模型的好坏取决于它们的输入。

sentences.en: It’s only through a system like data dignity that we can expand the models into new frontiers.
sentences.zh: 只有通过像数据尊严这样的系统，我们才能将模型扩展到新的领域。

sentences.zh: 现在，获得 L.L.M. 学位要容易得多。

sentences.en: Why not solve that problem by giving people who add more virtual worlds a chance for prestige and income?
sentences.zh: 为什么不给那些添加更多虚拟世界的人一个获得声望和收入的机会来解决这个问题呢？

blocks.html_en: Could data dignity help with any of the human-annihilation scenarios? A big model could make us incompetent, or confuse us so much that our society goes collectively off the rails; a powerful, malevolent person could use A.I. to do us all great harm; and some people also think that the model itself could “jailbreak,” taking control of our machines or weapons and using them against us.

sentences.en: Could data dignity help with any of the human-annihilation scenarios?
sentences.zh: 数据尊严可以帮助解决任何人类灭绝的情况吗？

sentences.en: A big model could make us incompetent, or confuse us so much that our society goes collectively off the rails; a powerful, malevolent person could use A.I.
sentences.zh: 一个大的模型可能会让我们变得无能，或者让我们感到非常困惑，以至于我们的社会集体脱轨；一个强大的、恶毒的人可以使用人工智能。

sentences.en: to do us all great harm; and some people also think that the model itself could “jailbreak,” taking control of our machines or weapons and using them against us.
sentences.zh: 对我们所有人造成巨大伤害；有些人还认为该模型本身可以“越狱”，控制我们的机器或武器并用它们来对付我们。

blocks.html_en: We can find precedents for some of these scenarios not just in science fiction but in more ordinary market and technology failures. An example is the 2019 catastrophe related to Boeing’s 737 MAX jets. The planes included a flight-path-correction feature that in some cases fought the pilots, causing two mass-casualty crashes. The problem was not the technology in isolation but the way that it was integrated into the sales cycle, training sessions, user interface, and documentation. Pilots thought that they were doing the right thing by trying to counteract the system in certain circumstances, but they were doing exactly the wrong thing, and they had no way of knowing. Boeing failed to communicate clearly about how the technology worked, and the resulting confusion led to disaster.

sentences.en: We can find precedents for some of these scenarios not just in science fiction but in more ordinary market and technology failures.
sentences.zh: 我们不仅可以在科幻小说中找到其中一些场景的先例，而且可以在更常见的市场和技术失败中找到先例。

sentences.en: An example is the 2019 catastrophe related to Boeing’s 737 MAX jets.
sentences.zh: 2019 年与波音 737 MAX 喷气式飞机相关的灾难就是一个例子。

sentences.zh: 这些飞机具有飞行路径校正功能，在某些情况下会与飞行员发生冲突，导致两起大规模人员伤亡的坠机事故。

sentences.en: The problem was not the technology in isolation but the way that it was integrated into the sales cycle, training sessions, user interface, and documentation.
sentences.zh: 问题不在于孤立的技术，而在于将其集成到销售周期、培训课程、用户界面和文档中的方式。

sentences.en: Pilots thought that they were doing the right thing by trying to counteract the system in certain circumstances, but they were doing exactly the wrong thing, and they had no way of knowing.
sentences.zh: 飞行员认为他们在某些情况下试图抵消系统的作用是正确的，但他们做的完全是错误的，而且他们无从得知。

sentences.en: Boeing failed to communicate clearly about how the technology worked, and the resulting confusion led to disaster.
sentences.zh: 波音公司未能清楚地传达该技术的工作原理，由此产生的混乱导致了灾难。

blocks.html_en: Anything engineered—cars, bridges, buildings—can cause harm to people, and yet we have built a civilization on engineering. It’s by increasing and broadening human awareness, responsibility, and participation that we can make automation safe; conversely, if we treat our inventions as occult objects, we can hardly be good engineers. Seeing A.I. as a form of social collaboration is more actionable: it gives us access to the engine room, which is made of people.

sentences.en: Anything engineered—cars, bridges, buildings—can cause harm to people, and yet we have built a civilization on engineering.
sentences.zh: 任何工程设计的东西——汽车、桥梁、建筑——都可能对人类造成伤害，但我们却建立了一个以工程为基础的文明。

sentences.en: It’s by increasing and broadening human awareness, responsibility, and participation that we can make automation safe; conversely, if we treat our inventions as occult objects, we can hardly be good engineers.
sentences.zh: 通过提高和扩大人类的意识、责任和参与，我们可以使自动化变得安全；相反，如果我们将我们的发明视为神秘的物体，我们就很难成为优秀的工程师。

sentences.en: Seeing A.I.
sentences.zh: 看到 A.I.

sentences.en: as a form of social collaboration is more actionable: it gives us access to the engine room, which is made of people.
sentences.zh: 作为一种更具可操作性的社会协作形式：它使我们能够进入由人组成的机房。

blocks.html_en: Let’s consider the apocalyptic scenario in which A.I. drives our society off the rails. One way this could happen is through deepfakes. Suppose that an evil person, perhaps working in an opposing government on a war footing, decides to stoke mass panic by sending all of us convincing videos of our loved ones being tortured or abducted from our homes. (The data necessary to create such videos are, in many cases, easy to obtain through social media or other channels.) Chaos would ensue, even if it soon became clear that the videos were faked. How could we prevent such a scenario? The answer is obvious: digital information must have context. Any collection of bits needs a history. When you lose context, you lose control.

sentences.en: Let’s consider the apocalyptic scenario in which A.I.
sentences.zh: 让我们考虑一下人工智能的世界末日场景。

sentences.en: drives our society off the rails.
sentences.zh: 让我们的社会脱轨。

sentences.en: One way this could happen is through deepfakes.
sentences.zh: 发生这种情况的一种方法是通过深度伪造。

sentences.zh: 假设一个邪恶的人，可能在战争状态下在敌对政府工作，决定向我们所有人发送令人信服的亲人在家里遭受酷刑或绑架的视频，从而引发大规模恐慌。

sentences.en: (The data necessary to create such videos are, in many cases, easy to obtain through social media or other channels.)
sentences.zh: （在许多情况下，创建此类视频所需的数据很容易通过社交媒体或其他渠道获得。）

sentences.en: Chaos would ensue, even if it soon became clear that the videos were faked.
sentences.zh: 即使很快就发现这些视频是伪造的，混乱也会随之而来。

sentences.en: How could we prevent such a scenario?
sentences.zh: 我们怎样才能防止这种情况发生呢？

sentences.en: The answer is obvious: digital information must have context.
sentences.zh: 答案很明显：数字信息必须有上下文。

sentences.en: Any collection of bits needs a history.
sentences.zh: 任何位的集合都需要历史。

sentences.en: When you lose context, you lose control.
sentences.zh: 当你失去背景时，你就会失去控制。

blocks.html_en: Why don’t bits come attached to the stories of their origins? There are many reasons. The original design of the Web didn’t keep track of where bits came from, likely to make it easier for the network to grow quickly. (Computers and bandwidth were poor in the beginning.) Why didn’t we start remembering where bits came from when it became more feasible to at least approximate digital provenance? It always felt to me that we wanted the Web to be more mysterious than it needed to be. Whatever the reason, the Web was made to remember everything while forgetting its context.

sentences.en: Why don’t bits come attached to the stories of their origins?
sentences.zh: 为什么比特不与它们的起源故事相关联？

sentences.en: There are many reasons.
sentences.zh: 原因有很多。

sentences.en: The original design of the Web didn’t keep track of where bits came from, likely to make it easier for the network to grow quickly.
sentences.zh: 网络的最初设计并没有跟踪比特的来源，这可能会让网络更容易快速增长。

sentences.en: (Computers and bandwidth were poor in the beginning.)
sentences.zh: （一开始计算机和带宽都很差。）

sentences.en: Why didn’t we start remembering where bits came from when it became more feasible to at least approximate digital provenance?
sentences.zh: 当至少近似数字来源变得更加可行时，为什么我们不开始记住比特的来源呢？

sentences.en: It always felt to me that we wanted the Web to be more mysterious than it needed to be.
sentences.zh: 我总觉得我们希望网络比它需要的更加神秘。

sentences.en: Whatever the reason, the Web was made to remember everything while forgetting its context.
sentences.zh: 无论出于何种原因，网络都是为了记住一切而忘记其上下文。

blocks.html_en: Today, most people take it for granted that the Web, and indeed the Internet it is built on, is, by its nature, anti-contextual and devoid of provenance. We assume that decontextualization is intrinsic to the very idea of a digital network. That was never so, however; the initial proposals for digital-network architecture, put forward by the monumental scientist Vannevar Bush in 1945 and the computer scientist Ted Nelson in 1960, preserved provenance. Now A.I. is revealing the true costs of ignoring this approach. Without provenance, we have no way of controlling our A.I.s, or of making them economically fair. And this risks pushing our society to the brink.

sentences.en: Today, most people take it for granted that the Web, and indeed the Internet it is built on, is, by its nature, anti-contextual and devoid of provenance.
sentences.zh: 今天，大多数人理所当然地认为网络，以及它所建立的互联网，就其本质而言，是反语境的且缺乏出处。

sentences.en: We assume that decontextualization is intrinsic to the very idea of a digital network.
sentences.zh: 我们假设去情境化是数字网络理念所固有的。

sentences.en: That was never so, however; the initial proposals for digital-network architecture, put forward by the monumental scientist Vannevar Bush in 1945 and the computer scientist Ted Nelson in 1960, preserved provenance.
sentences.zh: 然而事实并非如此。伟大的科学家 Vannevar Bush 于 1945 年和计算机科学家 Ted Nelson 于 1960 年提出的数字网络架构的最初建议保留了出处。

sentences.en: Now A.I.
sentences.zh: 现在A.I.

sentences.en: is revealing the true costs of ignoring this approach.
sentences.zh: 揭示了忽视这种方法的真正成本。

sentences.en: Without provenance, we have no way of controlling our A.I.s, or of making them economically fair.
sentences.zh: 如果没有来源，我们就无法控制我们的人工智能，也无法使它们在经济上公平。

sentences.en: And this risks pushing our society to the brink.
sentences.zh: 这有可能将我们的社会推向崩溃的边缘。

blocks.html_en: If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why? Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera. We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it. Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available? There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.

sentences.en: If a chatbot appears to be manipulative, mean, weird, or deceptive, what kind of answer do we want when we ask why?
sentences.zh: 如果聊天机器人看起来具有操纵性、卑鄙、怪异或欺骗性，当我们问为什么时，我们想要什么样的答案？

sentences.en: Revealing the indispensable antecedent examples from which the bot learned its behavior would provide an explanation: we’d learn that it drew on a particular work of fan fiction, say, or a soap opera.
sentences.zh: 揭示机器人学习其行为所必需的先例将提供一个解释：我们会了解到它借鉴了某部同人小说或肥皂剧的特定作品。

sentences.en: We could react to that output differently, and adjust the inputs of the model to improve it.
sentences.zh: 我们可以对该输出做出不同的反应，并调整模型的输入以改进它。

sentences.en: Why shouldn’t that type of explanation always be available?
sentences.zh: 为什么这种类型的解释不应该总是可用的？

sentences.en: There may be cases in which provenance shouldn’t be revealed, so as to give priority to privacy—but provenance will usually be more beneficial to individuals and society than an exclusive commitment to privacy would be.
sentences.zh: 在某些情况下，不应透露出处，以便优先考虑隐私，但出处通常比对隐私的排他性承诺对个人和社会更有利。

blocks.html_en: The technical challenges of data dignity are real and must inspire serious scientific ambition. The policy challenges would also be substantial—a sign, perhaps, that they are meaningful and concrete. But we need to change the way we think, and to embrace the hard work of renovation. By persisting with the ideas of the past—among them, a fascination with the possibility of an A.I. that lives independently of the people who contribute to it—we risk using our new technologies in ways that make the world worse. If society, economics, culture, technology, or any other spheres of activity are to serve people, that can only be because we decide that people enjoy a special status to be served.

sentences.en: The technical challenges of data dignity are real and must inspire serious scientific ambition.
sentences.zh: 数据尊严的技术挑战是真实存在的，必须激发严肃的科学雄心。

content_html_zh:
控制新技术的方法有很多，但首先我们必须停止神话化它。

April 20, 2023

尼古拉斯·康拉德/《纽约客》插图

作为一名计算机科学家，我不喜欢“人工智能”这个词。 事实上，我认为这是一种误导——甚至可能有点危险。 每个人都已经在使用这个术语，现在争论这个词似乎有点晚了。 但我们正处于一个新的技术时代的开端——对技术管理不善的最简单方法就是误解它。

“人工智能”这个术语有着悠久的历史——它是在 20 世纪 50 年代计算机出现的早期创造的。 最近，计算机科学家是在《终结者》和《黑客帝国》等电影以及《星际迷航：下一代》中的指挥官数据等角色中成长起来的。 这些文化试金石已经成为科技文化中近乎宗教的神话。 计算机科学家渴望创造人工智能是很自然的事情。 并实现长久以来的梦想。

然而，令人惊讶的是，许多追求人工智能的人。 梦还担心这可能意味着人类的末日。 人们普遍认为，即使是当今最核心的科学家，人工智能也能做到这一点。 研究人员正在做的事情可能会很快导致我们物种的灭绝，或者至少对人类造成巨大伤害。 在最近的一项民意调查中，一半的 A.I. 科学家们一致认为，人类至少有百分之十的可能性会被人工智能毁灭。 就连我的同事兼朋友、OpenAI 的负责人 Sam Altman 也发表了类似的评论。 走进任何一家硅谷咖啡馆，你都会听到同样的争论：一个人说新代码只是代码，人们负责，但另一个人则认为持这种观点的人只是不明白新技术有多深刻。 这些论点并不完全合理：当我请我最可怕的科学家朋友阐明人工智能是如何运作的。 世界末日可能会发生，他们常常因为试图想象无限的人而陷入瘫痪。 他们说“加速进步将飞逝而过，我们将无法想象正在发生的事情。”

我不同意这种说话方式。 我的许多朋友和同事对 GPT-4 等最新大型模型的体验印象深刻，并且几乎在守夜等待更深层智能的出现。 我的立场不是他们错了，而是我们不能确定；我们保留以不同方式对软件进行分类的选项。

最务实的立场是考虑人工智能。 作为一种工具，而不是一种生物。 我的态度并不能消除危险的可能性：无论我们怎么想，我们仍然可以糟糕地设计和操作我们的新技术，从而伤害我们，甚至导致我们的灭绝。 神话化这项技术只会让我们更有可能无法很好地运用它——而这种思维限制了我们的想象力，将它们与昨天的梦想联系在一起。 假设不存在人工智能这样的东西，我们可以更好地工作。 我们越早理解这一点，我们就能越早开始智能地管理我们的新技术。

如果新技术不是真正的人工智能，那么它是什么？ 在我看来，理解我们今天正在建设的东西的最准确方法是将其视为一种创新的社会协作形式。

像 OpenAI 的 GPT-4 这样的程序可以按顺序编写句子，它就像维基百科的一个版本，其中包含更多数据，并使用统计数据混合在一起。 按订单创建图像的程序类似于在线图像搜索的一个版本，但具有组合图片的系统。 在这两种情况下，都是人编写文本并提供图像。 新程序融合了人类思维所做的工作。 创新之处在于，混搭过程已经受到引导和约束，因此结果可用且往往引人注目。 这是一项重大成就，值得庆祝，但它可以被认为是阐明了人类创造之间先前隐藏的一致性，而不是新思维的发明。

据我所知，我的观点是对这项技术的恭维。 毕竟，除了社会合作之外，文明是什么？ 看到 A.I. 作为一种合作方式，而不是作为一种创造独立智能生物的技术，可能会使其不那么神秘——不像 HAL 9000 或 Commander Data。 但这很好，因为神秘只会增加管理不善的可能性。

人们很容易将智能归因于新系统；它们具有我们通常不会将其与计算机技术联系在一起的灵活性和不可预测性。 但这种灵活性源于简单的数学。 像 GPT-4 这样的大型语言模型包含特定单词在程序处理的大量文本中如何重合的累积记录。 这种庞大的列表使系统本质上近似许多语法模式，以及所谓的作者风格的各个方面。 当您输入按特定顺序包含某些单词的查询时，您的输入与模型中的内容相关联；由于关联数十亿个条目的复杂性，每次结果都可能略有不同。

这个过程的非重复性可以让人感觉很生动。 从某种意义上说，它可以使新系统更加以人为本。 当你用 A.I. 合成新图像时 tool, you may get a bunch of similar options and then have to choose from them;如果您是使用 L.L.M. 学位的学生 要在论文作业中作弊，您可以阅读模型生成的选项并选择一个。 不可重复的技术需要人类做出一点选择。

A.I. 的许多用途 我喜欢依靠当计算机变得不那么严格时我们获得的优势。 正如我们所知，数字化的东西具有脆弱的品质，迫使人们遵守它，而不是评估它。 我们都经历过看着医生办公室里的一些可怜的人努力在前台屏幕上完成预期的事情的痛苦。 脸部扭曲；人性受到损害。 符合数字化设计的需要创造了一种人类屈从的环境期望。 对人工智能的积极影响 如果我们好好利用它，它可能会结束这种折磨。 我们现在可以想象一个可以为色盲者动态重新设计的网站，或者一个可以根据某人特定的认知能力和风格进行定制的网站。 像我这样的人文主义者希望人们拥有更多的控制权，而不是受到技术的过度影响或指导。 灵活性可能会给我们带来一些影响力。

尽管如此，尽管有这些可能的好处，我们还是有理由担心新技术会以我们不喜欢或不理解的方式推动我们。 最近，我的一些朋友散发了一份请愿书，要求暂停最雄心勃勃的人工智能项目。 发展。 我们的想法是在暂停期间制定政策。 请愿书由我们社区中的一些人签署，但其他人没有签署。 我发现这个概念太模糊了——进展到什么程度才意味着暂停可以结束？ 每周，我都会收到来自寻求启动人工智能设置流程的组织的新的但总是模糊的使命声明。 政策。

这些努力的初衷是好的，但对我来说似乎毫无希望。 多年来，我一直致力于欧盟的隐私政策，我逐渐意识到我们不知道隐私是什么。 这是我们每天都会使用的一个术语，它在上下文中是有意义的，但我们无法很好地确定它以进行概括。 我们对隐私的最接近定义可能是“不受干扰的权利”，但在我们不断依赖数字服务的时代，这似乎很奇怪。 在人工智能的背景下，“不被计算操纵的权利”似乎几乎是正确的，但并没有完全说出我们想要的一切。

人工智能政策对话主要以“结盟”（人工智能的定义）等术语为主。 “想要”与人类想要的一致？）、“安全”（我们能否预见能够阻止坏人工智能的护栏？）和“公平”（我们能否阻止程序可能以不利于某些人的方式对待某些人？）。 通过追求这些想法，社区确实取得了很大的成就，但这并没有消除我们的恐惧。 我们最终会激励人们试图规避我们设置的模糊保护措施。 尽管保护措施确实有帮助，但整个事情变成了一场游戏——就像试图智胜狡猾的精灵一样。 结果是，人工智能研究界发出了警告，称他们的创造物可能很快就会杀死全人类，同时提出了更加紧迫但浮夸的审议程序。

最近，我尝试了一个非正式的实验，打电话给同事，询问他们是否有任何我们似乎都同意的具体内容。 我发现这是有共识的基础。 我们似乎都同意深度伪造——虚假但看似真实的图像、视频等——应该由创建它们的程序贴上这样的标签。 来自人造人的通信以及旨在操纵人类思维或行为的自动化交互也应该被标记。 我们也同意这些标签应该附带可以采取的行动。 人们应该能够理解他们所看到的，并应该有合理的选择作为回报。

这一切如何才能做到呢？ 我发现，人们几乎一致认为，我们当前人工智能的黑匣子性质。 工具必须结束。 系统必须变得更加透明。 我们需要更好地了解他们内心正在发生什么以及为什么。 这并不容易。 问题是大型模型 A.I. 我们正在谈论的系统并不是由明确的想法组成的。 系统“想要什么”没有明确的表示，也没有标签说明它何时做某件事，比如操纵一个人。 只有一片巨大的果冻海洋——巨大的数学混合。 一个作家权利组织提议，当在剧本写作过程中使用 GPT 等工具时，真正的人类作者应获得全额报酬；毕竟，该系统正在借鉴真人制作的脚本。 但当我们使用 A.I. 要制作电影剪辑，甚至可能是整部电影，不一定有编剧阶段。 一部电影可能看起来有剧本、配乐等，但它是作为一个整体被计算出来的。 同样，在从插图人工智能生成绘画之前也没有草图。 试图通过让系统吐出不必要的项目（如脚本、草图或意图）来打开黑匣子，将涉及构建另一个黑匣子来解释第一个黑匣子——这是一种无限的回归。

与此同时，大型模型的内部并不一定是无路可走的荒野。 从形式、计算的角度来看，我们可能不知道“想法”是什么，但可能存在不是由想法而是由人构成的轨迹。 在过去的某个时刻，一个真人创建了一个插图，并将其作为数据输入到模型中，并结合其他人的贡献，将其转化为新的图像。 大模型人工智能 是由人组成的——而打开黑匣子的方法就是揭露他们。

我参与开发的这个概念通常被称为“数据尊严”。 早在大型“人工智能”兴起之前，它就出现了，作为人们熟悉的安排的替代方案，在这种安排中，人们免费提供数据以换取免费服务，例如互联网搜索或社交网络。 数据尊严有时被称为“数据即劳动”或“多元化研究”。 事实证明，这种熟悉的安排有一个阴暗面：由于“网络效应”，一些平台占据了主导地位，淘汰了当地报纸等较小的参与者。 更糟糕的是，由于即时在线体验应该是免费的，唯一剩下的生意就是兜售影响力。 用户体验到的似乎是一个社群天堂，但他们却成为隐秘且令人上瘾的算法的目标，这些算法使人虚荣、烦躁和偏执。

在一个具有数据尊严的世界中，数字物品通常会与那些希望因其创造者而闻名的人类联系在一起。 在这个想法的某些版本中，人们可以从他们创造的东西中获得报酬，即使它是通过大模型过滤和重新组合的，而技术中心将通过促进人们想做的事情来赚取费用。 有些人对网上资本主义的想法感到震惊，但这将是一个更诚实的资本主义。 熟悉的“免费”安排是一场灾难。

科技界担心人工智能的原因之一。 可能存在的威胁在于它可以被用来玩弄人类，就像之前的数字技术浪潮一样。 考虑到这些新系统的力量和潜在影响力，担心可能导致灭绝并不是没有道理的。 由于这种危险已被广泛认识到，大型人工智能的到来。 可能是一个重新调整科技行业格局、使其变得更好的机会。

实现数据尊严需要技术研究和政策创新。 从这个意义上说，这个主题让我作为一名科学家感到兴奋。 打开黑匣子只会让模型变得更有趣。 它可能会帮助我们更多地了解语言，这是真正令人印象深刻的人类发明，也是我们在数十万年后仍在探索的语言。

数据尊严能否解决人们对人工智能经常表达的经济担忧？ 主要担心的是工人的价值会贬值或流离失所。 在公开场合，技术人员有时会说，在未来几年里，从事人工智能工作的人会 将提高生产力，并在生产力更高的经济中找到新类型的工作。 （一名工人可能会成为人工智能的快速工程师。 例如，与人工智能合作或控制人工智能的人） 然而，私下里，这些人经常会说：“不，人工智能。 将超越这种合作的想法。” 今天的会计师、放射科医生、卡车司机、作家、电影导演或音乐家不再有报酬。

当大模型提供有价值的输出时，数据尊严方法将追踪最独特和最有影响力的贡献者。 例如，如果你要求一个模型“我的孩子们在油画世界中冒险的会说话的猫的动画电影”，那么某些重要的油画家、猫肖像画家、配音演员和作家——或者他们的财产——可能会被认为对新杰作的创作至关重要。 他们会得到认可和激励。 他们甚至可能会得到报酬。

有一个刚刚起步的数据尊严研究社区，这里有一个内部辩论的例子：数据尊严应该尝试多详细的核算？ 并非所有人都同意。 该系统不一定会考虑到为大型模型做出环境贡献的数十亿人——例如，那些通过语法为模型的模拟能力添加了内容的人。 起初，数据尊严可能只关注特定情况下出现的少数特殊贡献者。 不过，随着时间的推移，随着中间权利组织（工会、行会、专业团体等）开始发挥作用，更多的人可能会被纳入其中。 数据尊严社区的人们有时将这些预期群体称为个人数据调解者 (MID) 或数据信任。 人们需要集体谈判权才能在网络世界中发挥价值，尤其是当他们可能迷失在巨大的人工智能中时。 模型。 当人们在一个团体中分担责任时，他们就会进行自我监督，从而减少政府和公司从上面进行审查或控制的需要或诱惑。 承认大模型的人性本质可能会导致新的积极社会制度的蓬勃发展。

数据尊严不仅仅属于白领角色。 考虑一下如果引入人工智能驱动的树木修剪机器人会发生什么。 人类树木修剪者可能会发现自己的价值被贬低，甚至失业。 但机器人最终可能会带来一种新型的间接景观艺术。 一些前工人或其他人可能会创造出创造性的方法——例如，从不同角度看起来不同的全息修剪——从而找到进入树木修剪模型的方法。 有了数据尊严，这些模型可能会创造新的收入来源，并通过集体组织分配。 随着时间的推移，树木修剪将变得更加多功能和有趣；将会有一个社区被激励去保持价值。 每一次成功引入人工智能。 或者机器人应用可能涉及一种新型创造性工作的启动。 无论大小，这都有助于轻松过渡到模型融入的经济。

硅谷的许多人将全民基本收入视为人工智能造成的潜在经济问题的解决方案。 但 U.B.I. 这相当于让每个人都领取救济金，以保留黑盒人工智能的理念。 我认为，这是一个可怕的想法，部分原因是坏人会想要夺取普遍福利体系中的权力中心，就像每一次共产主义实验一样。 我怀疑数据尊严能否发展到足以维持整个社会，但我怀疑任何社会或经济原则是否都将是完整的。 只要有可能，目标至少应该是建立一个新的创意类，而不是一个新的依赖类。

人工智能也有非利他的原因。 企业拥抱数据尊严。 模型的好坏取决于它们的输入。 只有通过像数据尊严这样的系统，我们才能将模型扩展到新的领域。 现在，获得 L.L.M. 学位要容易得多。 写一篇文章比要求程序生成一个交互式虚拟现实世界更重要，因为存在的虚拟世界很少。 为什么不给那些添加更多虚拟世界的人一个获得声望和收入的机会来解决这个问题呢？

数据尊严可以帮助解决任何人类灭绝的情况吗？ 一个大的模型可能会让我们变得无能，或者让我们感到非常困惑，以至于我们的社会集体脱轨；一个强大的、恶毒的人可以使用人工智能。 对我们所有人造成巨大伤害；有些人还认为该模型本身可以“越狱”，控制我们的机器或武器并用它们来对付我们。

我们不仅可以在科幻小说中找到其中一些场景的先例，而且可以在更常见的市场和技术失败中找到先例。 2019 年与波音 737 MAX 喷气式飞机相关的灾难就是一个例子。 这些飞机具有飞行路径校正功能，在某些情况下会与飞行员发生冲突，导致两起大规模人员伤亡的坠机事故。 问题不在于孤立的技术，而在于将其集成到销售周期、培训课程、用户界面和文档中的方式。 飞行员认为他们在某些情况下试图抵消系统的作用是正确的，但他们做的完全是错误的，而且他们无从得知。 波音公司未能清楚地传达该技术的工作原理，由此产生的混乱导致了灾难。

任何工程设计的东西——汽车、桥梁、建筑——都可能对人类造成伤害，但我们却建立了一个以工程为基础的文明。 通过提高和扩大人类的意识、责任和参与，我们可以使自动化变得安全；相反，如果我们将我们的发明视为神秘的物体，我们就很难成为优秀的工程师。 看到 A.I. 作为一种更具可操作性的社会协作形式：它使我们能够进入由人组成的机房。

让我们考虑一下人工智能的世界末日场景。 让我们的社会脱轨。 发生这种情况的一种方法是通过深度伪造。 假设一个邪恶的人，可能在战争状态下在敌对政府工作，决定向我们所有人发送令人信服的亲人在家里遭受酷刑或绑架的视频，从而引发大规模恐慌。 （在许多情况下，创建此类视频所需的数据很容易通过社交媒体或其他渠道获得。） 即使很快就发现这些视频是伪造的，混乱也会随之而来。 我们怎样才能防止这种情况发生呢？ 答案很明显：数字信息必须有上下文。 任何位的集合都需要历史。 当你失去背景时，你就会失去控制。

为什么比特不与它们的起源故事相关联？ 原因有很多。 网络的最初设计并没有跟踪比特的来源，这可能会让网络更容易快速增长。 （一开始计算机和带宽都很差。） 当至少近似数字来源变得更加可行时，为什么我们不开始记住比特的来源呢？ 我总觉得我们希望网络比它需要的更加神秘。 无论出于何种原因，网络都是为了记住一切而忘记其上下文。

今天，大多数人理所当然地认为网络，以及它所建立的互联网，就其本质而言，是反语境的且缺乏出处。 我们假设去情境化是数字网络理念所固有的。 然而事实并非如此。伟大的科学家 Vannevar Bush 于 1945 年和计算机科学家 Ted Nelson 于 1960 年提出的数字网络架构的最初建议保留了出处。 现在A.I. 揭示了忽视这种方法的真正成本。 如果没有来源，我们就无法控制我们的人工智能，也无法使它们在经济上公平。 这有可能将我们的社会推向崩溃的边缘。

如果聊天机器人看起来具有操纵性、卑鄙、怪异或欺骗性，当我们问为什么时，我们想要什么样的答案？ 揭示机器人学习其行为所必需的先例将提供一个解释：我们会了解到它借鉴了某部同人小说或肥皂剧的特定作品。 我们可以对该输出做出不同的反应，并调整模型的输入以改进它。 为什么这种类型的解释不应该总是可用的？ 在某些情况下，不应透露出处，以便优先考虑隐私，但出处通常比对隐私的排他性承诺对个人和社会更有利。

数据尊严的技术挑战是真实存在的，必须激发严肃的科学雄心。 政策挑战也将是巨大的——这也许表明它们是有意义和具体的。 但我们需要改变思维方式，拥抱改造的艰苦努力。 通过坚持过去的想法——其中包括对人工智能可能性的迷恋。 它独立于为其做出贡献的人而存在——我们冒着使用新技术而使世界变得更糟的风险。 如果社会、经济、文化、技术或任何其他活动领域要为人服务，那只能是因为我们决定人享有被服务的特殊地位。

这是我对所有同事的恳求。 想想人。 人是比特问题的答案。 ❖
